segments.s439-g17: "گه آمد که ... زمان" is an idiomatic conditional clause: گه آمد (literally 'if/when it comes') introduces "that if it happens that...". The relative particle که links the clause to the verb phrase that follows; بر تو سرآید is a poetic, impersonal phrasing meaning 'befall you' (بر + pronoun + سرآید), and زمان ('the time') completes the sense — word order is somewhat inverted for emphasis (condition first, then the time).
segments.s439-g18: شوی + کشته forms a passive/resultative idea: شوی (from شدن) here functions as a 2nd-person verbal form meaning 'you will be / you shall be' (auxiliary sense) and کشته is a past participle 'killed' serving as the predicate complement. در is the preposition 'in', دام 'trap', and آهرمنان is the plural/name 'Ahrimans' (devils) making a genitive sense 'the trap of the Ahrimans'.
segments.s439-g19: هم‌انگه is a temporal adverb 'at that moment'. سپهدار کابل is a nominal phrase 'the commander of Kabul' (title + place). ز راه is a prepositional phrase 'from the road' placed after the title for rhythmic/poetic order.
segments.s439-g20: ز دشت (from the plain) marks origin; اندر آمد is a poetic verb phrase 'came in/entered' where اندر is an older prepositional/adverbial element 'into/in' and آمد is 'came'. به introduces the destination 'to/into' and نخچیرگاه is 'hunting-ground'. Word order reflects classical/epic Persian style.
segments.s439-g21: گو here is a narrative particle (literally 'say' / 'it is said') introducing the reported perception; پیلتن را uses را as the definite/direct-object marker, so the phrase means 'he saw Piltan so/that tired' (چنان = 'so/thus', خسته = 'tired'), with دید the past tense 'saw'.
segments.s439-g22: همان 'that very/the same' points back to the preceding state; خستگیهاش is 'his weariness' (خستگی + possessive -اش), نابسته is an older/poetic adjective 'unbound/loose' (here describing the condition of the weariness), and دید again is 'he saw' — the second clause restates what was perceived, with a poetic, compact syntax.
segments.s439-g23: بدو گفت is an archaic/epic phrasing for 'he said to him' (بدو = به + او, 'to him'); کای is a vocative particle 'O' (used in address), and نامدار سپاه is a title-like phrase 'renowned of the army' = 'renowned commander of the army' (epic apposition).
segments.s439-g24: چه is the interrogative 'what'; بودت is the old 2nd-person past imperfect/past form meaning 'you were / you had been' (بود + -ت enclitic); برین is written as بر + ین (an older/orthographic form equivalent to 'this' or 'on this') giving 'on this plain'; دشت is 'plain' and نخچیرگاه 'hunting-ground' indicates the location being questioned. The clause literally asks 'what were you on this plain, [on] the hunting-ground?'.
segments.s439-g25: شوم is an archaic/poetic 1st‑person form (optative/subjunctive) used here to introduce a future action ('I will'). زود is an adverb 'soon/quickly'. چندی modifies پزشک and means 'a few/several'. آورم is the 1st‑person present/subjunctive of آوردن, the principal verb 'I will bring' placed at the end of the clause in poetic word order.
segments.s439-g26: ز is the preposition 'from / because of'. درد تو is a genitive phrase 'your pain' (درد + enclitic تو). خونین modifies سرشک and means 'blood‑stained'; سرشک ('tear, teardrop') is the noun; آورم again is 1st‑person 'I will bring' used idiomatically here ('I will shed/bring forth tears').
segments.s439-g27: مگر here functions as the conditional 'unless'. خستگیهات is 'your fatigues / your weariness' (خستگی‌ها + second‑person enclitic -ت). گردد is subjunctive/passive 'become / be made' and درست is an adjective/adverb 'right / well' that complements گردد: 'become right / be healed.'
segments.s439-g28: نباید is an impersonal modal 'one must not / you should not'. م + را in the line together function as مرا ('me' / 'my') — the text separates the syllables but grammatically form the object 'm e' marked by را. رخ is 'face'. به is the preposition indicating instrument/means ('with / in'), here introducing خوناب 'blood‑water' (bloody discharge). شست is the past form of شستن ('washed'); with نباید it yields the prohibition 'should not wash' (poetic verb‑last order).
segments.s439-g29: تهمتن is the proper name/epithet (Tahmātan). چنین is the adverb 'thus/so' modifying the speech act. داد is the past tense 'gave' and پاسخ is its object 'an answer / reply'. بدوی is an archaic/poetic particle here marking the act of replying or 'to him / in return' (usage is old/poetic).
segments.s439-g30: که‌ای combines the clause‑introducing که with the vocative ای and here functions to lead into the direct address ('saying: “O ...”'). مرد is the vocative 'man / fellow' (can also mean 'hero' in epic diction). بدگوهر is a descriptive compound 'bad‑gem' → 'ill‑natured / of bad character'. چاره‌جوی is a compound noun 'seeker of remedy' (چاره + جو 'seeker').
segments.s439-g31: سر آمد is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'has come / the time has arrived'. مرا (written here split as م + را elsewhere) is the indirect object 'to me / for me'. روزگار is the subject 'time / fate / days'. پزشک at the end is a vocative address 'O physician' (word order is poetic: verb phrase + indirect object + subject + vocative).
segments.s439-g32: تو is the 2nd‑person subject 'you'. بر من is the prepositional phrase 'upon me / over me'. مپالای is an obscure/ dialectal/poetic verb form (here functioning as a negative jussive/imperative: 'do not ... / do not smear/splash'); its exact ordinary modern equivalent is not preserved. خونین modifies سرشک 'blood‑stained tear(s)' which is the object; the verb governs that object ('do not (let) bloody tears fall upon me').
segments.s439-g81: مرا زور دادی که is a clause meaning 'you forced me that...'. مرا is the object 'me' with the enclitic را (object marker). زور here is a noun 'force/compulsion' and دادی is the past 2nd‑person singular 'you gave/inflicted', so together 'you exerted force on me' (i.e. 'you forced me'). که introduces the following subordinate clause ('that/before...'). از مرگ پیش uses poetic order: از + مرگ + پیش = 'before death' (پیش as 'before').
segments.s439-g82: ازین بی‌وفا خواستم کین خویش: ازین (از + این) 'this, from this' + بی‌وفا 'the faithless/unfaithful one'. خواستم is past 'I wanted/sought'. کین خویش is the object 'my vengeance' or 'vengeance of mine' (خویش = 'one's own' realized as خوی + ش possessive). The whole segment means 'I sought (or desired) my vengeance on this faithless one.'
segments.s439-g83: بگفت این is 'he said this' (بگفت = 'he said/told', این = 'this' referring to the preceding words). و connects to the next clause. جانش is جان + ش, 'his life/soul', and برآمد here means 'issued forth/rose' (a common poetic verb for a soul leaving the body). ز تن = 'from the body' using poetic ز (variant of از).
segments.s439-g84: برو زار و گریان شدند انجمن has verb‑final subject: شدند (they became) governs predicates برو زار (deeply wretched/stricken) and گریان (weeping). انجمن ('the assembly') appears at the end as the subject: 'the assembly became stricken and wept.' The adjectives/states precede شدند in poetic order.
segments.s439-g85: زواره is a proper name/place. به چاهی دگر در بمرد literally 'into/at another well he died' — به ... در indicates motion/location 'to/in a well', دگر 'another'. بمرد is a past/jussive narrative form meaning 'he died' (here narratively 'he died'). Word order is poetic but means 'Zowāreh died in another well.'
segments.s439-g86: سواری نماند از بزرگان و خرد: نماند (past negative sense here) with سواری as the logical subject means 'no horseman/rider remained'. از بزرگان و خرد is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the great ones and the wise' i.e. 'among the nobles and the wise'. Poetic ellipsis leaves out an explicit negative particle but the sense is 'there remained none.'
segments.s439-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. با خستگی is a prepositional phrase 'with (a) weariness'; چشمها is the subject 'the eyes' (plural). برگشاد is a past/participial form 'opened' placed after the subject in poetic word order, so the whole clause reads 'when, with weariness, (his) eyes opened'.
segments.s439-g2: بدید is the past tense 'he saw'. آن before a noun is a demonstrative 'that/the'. بداندیش is an adjective/noun 'ill‑minded, malicious'. روی (here) means 'face', and شغاد is a proper name (Shaghad) — the name does not match the dictionary senses given, so it is a name in context. Word order (verb then object phrase) is typical of older/poetic Persian.
segments.s439-g3: بدانست is 'he knew/realized'. کان is an archaic/classical marker introducing the subordinate clause (here functioning like 'that'), used in older Persian — its exact dictionary senses listed do not match cleanly so it is treated as an archaic connective. چاره و راه اوست is the predicate 'was his remedy and way', with اوست the copula 'is/was' at the end (typical Persian copular order).
segments.s439-g4: This is a nominal predicate identifying Shaghad: شغاد is the subject (proper name, not covered by the provided senses), followed by two adjectival epithets فریبنده 'deceptive' and بدخواه 'ill‑wishing, malevolent', and the copula اوست 'is (to him)'. Adjectives follow the noun in Persian and stack before the copula.
segments.s439-g5: بدو is the dative clitic 'to him' (meaning 'said to him' when followed by گفت). گفت here functions as the past tense verb 'said' (the dictionary senses listed are for 'speech' rather than the verb form, so the exact verbal sense is not in the provided list). کای is the vocative 'O' addressing someone, followed by مرد 'man' and the two epithets بدبخت 'unfortunate' and شوم 'accursed/ill‑omened'.
segments.s439-g6: ز here introduces the cause 'because of / from'. کار تو is a genitive 'your deed(s)'. ویران شد is the verbal phrase 'became ruined' (شد = became, with ویران 'ruined' as predicate). آباد بوم 'prosperous homeland' is the noun phrase that has become ruined; poetry often inverts the normal order so the verb appears before the full subject phrase.
segments.s439-g7: پشیمانی آید is a nominal predicate with the verb 'آید' (will come) — 'regret will come'. ترا is the object/dative 'to you' (here written as ت + را in the word list). زین سخن is 'from these words' (زین = archaic/poetic fusion of از + این = 'from this/these', though the provided senses list does not include this use), so the clause means 'Regret will come to you from these words.'
segments.s439-g8: بپیچی is a jussive/imperative‑like form of پیچیدن 'to turn' (here 'turn away'). ازین = 'from this' (از + این; the tokenization shows از and ین separately, with ین as the orthographic variant of 'این'). بد is 'evil/bad'. نگردی is the negative subjunctive/optative of گردیدن 'to become' (ن‑ + گردی), 'may you not become' or 'do not become'. کهن is an adjective 'old/ancient'. Together the clause commands 'turn away from this evil; do not become old/ancient' (poetic admonition).
segments.s439-g49: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' introducing the source. ترکش is the noun 'quiver' (here written segmented), with the following clitic ش marking possession ('his'); برآور is an imperative (bring forth / take out). کمان مرا را is the definite, possessed object ('my bow') where مرا is the possessive clitic and را is the definite/object marker attached after the noun phrase.
segments.s439-g50: به کار آور is an imperative idiom: به + کار + آور literally 'bring to work/use' = 'employ, put to use'. آن ترجمان مرا را is the object phrase 'that translator/interpreter of mine' (آن = 'that', ترجمان = 'interpreter/expounder'), with مرا marking possession and را the definite/object marker; the whole segment therefore commands someone to employ that person/thing for the speaker.
segments.s439-g51: به زه کن is an imperative command: به + زه (bow‑string) + کن (imperative 'do') = 'string it' or 'attach the string'. بنه here is a verb 'put/place' (classical usage) followed by پیش من 'before me / in my presence'; با دو تیر 'with two arrows' indicates accompaniment or readiness. Word order is imperative verb + object/place phrase.
segments.s439-g52: نباید که is an impersonal negative modal construction meaning 'it must not be / it should not happen that...'. که introduces the subordinate clause (that / who), followed by آن شیر نخچیرگیر 'that lion, the hunter' as the subject of the subordinate clause. The verb is omitted here and implied by the following lines.
segments.s439-g53: ز دشت اندر آید is a circumstantial clause 'he/it comes in from the plain' where ز + noun gives the source and اندر آید is 'comes in/enters'. The following ز بهر شکار 'for the sake of the hunt' uses ز + بهر to indicate purpose ('for the hunt'). Word order is source → motion verb → purpose.
segments.s439-g54: من اینجا فتاده is a verbless/clipped clause meaning 'I have fallen here' (فتاده = 'fallen'). چنین is an adverb 'thus/so', and تن‌فگار is a nominal epithet (literally 'body‑piercer' / 'tormentor'); the sequence states the speaker's condition and labels it (subject + locative + participial adjective + epithet).
segments.s439-g55: ببیند is a third‑person present/jussive form 'if he sees' or 'let him see'. مرا (here split in the word list) is the object 'me', and زو means 'from him'; گزند آیدم literally 'harm comes to me' — گزند (harm) is the subject of آیدم (comes to me / befalls me). The clause expresses a conditional/result: 'If he sees me, harm will befall me from him.'
segments.s439-g56: کمانی بود سودمند آیدم is a short predicative sequence where کمانی ('of the bow' / 'bow‑like') qualifies an implied thing ('a bow'), بود functions as a modal/auxiliary giving a conditional or potential sense ('would be' / 'would be there'), and سودمند آیدم means 'would be useful to me' (آیدم = 'comes to me' = 'avail me'). Together: 'a bow would be useful to me.'
segments.s439-g33: فراوان نمانی سرآید زمان is structured as two clauses run together: فراوان نمانی ('you will not remain long') uses فراوان as an adverbial intensifier and نمانی is a present/future-directed form of ماندن with the negative prefix ن- attached (negating 'to remain'); سرآید زمان ('the time will come') places the verb سرآید after the noun زمان in the typical Persian word order 'subject/time + verb' and uses سرآید in the sense 'to come, arrive' to introduce the inevitable moment.
segments.s439-g34: کسی زنده برنگذرد باسمان is a single clause giving the consequence: کسی زنده ('no one alive') is the logical subject (here rendered negatively by the preceding context), برنگذرد is a present/future-oriented negative form meaning 'will not pass/escape', and باسمان is here a proper name attached without a preposition (poetic compression) indicating the agent/place/force — hence 'from Basman' or 'by Basman'. The verb follows the subject in normal Persian order and the negative is carried by بر- + نگذرد.
segments.s439-g35: نه من بیش دارم ز جمشید فر is a negated possession statement: نه negates the clause; من is the explicit first-person subject; بیش دارم uses بیش as 'more/greater' with دارم (I have) to say 'I do not have more'; ز introduces the comparison 'than/from Jamshid' and فر is the abstract noun 'farr' (royal glory) possessed by Jamshid.
segments.s439-g36: که ببرید بیور میانش به ار is a subordinate clause introduced by که ('that/so that' or 'namely'), where ببرید is used in an optative/hortative sense ('might take/seize' — a sense of بردن 'gain/take'), بیور is a proper name (the object/agent who would take), میانش ('in its/among them') is a locative/partitive phrase formed from میان + -ش, and به ار ends the clause with ار meaning 'if' (a conditional particle); the clause therefore gives a hypothetical result/limitation tied to the previous negation.
segments.s439-g37: نه از آفریدون وز کیقباد is a coordinated negation: نه ... از introduces 'not from', آفریدون is the proper name, and وز here is a poetic contracted sequence corresponding to و ز (and from) linking the second name کیقباد; syntactically this is a negative listing 'neither from Afridun nor from Kay Qobad.'
segments.s439-g38: بزرگان و شاهان فرخ‌نژاد lists the affected groups: بزرگان 'the great ones' (nominative/subjective plural), و is the coordinating 'and', شاهان 'kings', and فرخ‌نژاد is an adjectival/epithet 'well‑born' or 'of fortunate lineage' qualifying those kings; word order is noun + epithet as is common in Persian poetic diction.
segments.s439-g39: گلوی سیاوش به خنجر برید is a simple past passive/agentive clause in terse poetic diction: گلوی سیاوش ('Siavash's throat') is the patient/affected noun phrase, به خنجر uses به in the instrumental/agent sense 'with/by a dagger', and برید here denotes the act 'was cut' (past action). The verb follows the noun phrase as usual in Persian.
segments.s439-g40: گروی زره چون زمانش رسید is a temporal clause: چون زمانش رسید 'when his time arrived' is the adverbial temporal phrase (چون = 'when', زمانش = 'his time', رسید = 'arrived'), and گروی زره sits before it as a noun phrase meaning the thing associated with the armor — here rendered 'his cuirass/armor yielded' (grouping/verb omitted in terse verse). The exact lexical force of گروی is archaic/poetic and does not match the supplied senses exactly, so the gloss treats it as an armor‑related noun tied to زره.
segments.s439-g9: برو is an imperative (’go’). با+فرامرز is a prepositional phrase 'with Faramarz' attached to the verb. و links a second imperative: یکتاه باش is another imperative clause ('be single/sole-minded'); یکتاه is an adjective/adverbial quality placed before the verb باش (imperative 'be'), so the whole means 'go with Faramarz and be single-minded.'
segments.s439-g10: به جان و دل is an adverbial phrase 'with (your) life and heart' (literal 'to life and heart') intensifying devotion. او را is the object (ô + definite object marker را) meaning 'him'; نکوخواه is a substantive/adjective 'well-wisher' and باش is the imperative 'be', so syntactically: 'Be (باش) a well-wisher (نکوخواه) of him (او را), with your life and heart (به جان و دل).'
segments.s439-g11: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'; پاسخ (object) precedes the verb—a common poetic order—and آورد is past tense 'brought/produced' (3rd sg. past of آوردن). ناکس شغاد is the agent/appositive 'the wretched Shaghad' placed after the verb for emphasis: 'the wretch Shaghad produced such an answer.'
segments.s439-g12: که introduces a reported clause ('that'). گردون گردان is a compound epithet 'the revolving/turning sky/heavens' (گردون + adjectival/participle گردان). ترا را is the object 'you' (ترا is enclitic/object, with را for emphasis/definiteness). داد داد are past-tense dád forms; the duplication is a poetic intensification/repetition of the past verb 'gave' (آوردن/دادن) — overall: 'that the turning heavens gave you up.' The verb is past tense 3rd singular.
segments.s439-g13: تو (you) is the addressed subject. چندین چه is an emphatic/exclamatory combination ('how many, how often'); یازی (poetic verbal noun/verb) means 'to break forth/launch an attack' and is here qualified by the infinitive phrase به خون ریختن ('in shedding blood'), where ریختن is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to spill/shed'. The whole says essentially 'How many times have you broken forth, shedding blood...'.
segments.s439-g14: به هر سو is an adverbial phrase 'to every side/ everywhere' (به + هر + سو). به تاراج و آویختن are coordinated verbal-noun/infinitive complements: تاراج 'pillage' and آویختن 'to hang / hanging' (both describing actions accompanying the burst forth). The structure lists the attendant crimes: 'to every side — pillaging and hanging.'
segments.s439-g15: ز کابل is a prepositional phrase 'from Kabul'. نخوا هی is the poetic split form of نخواهی (negative future/impersonal negative) meaning 'you will not/will not wish' or 'you will no longer (take/seek)'; دگر بار is 'again' (دگر + بار, 'another time') and سیم (text after the listed words) is the object 'silver' understood as the thing sought/taken. Word order puts the negated verb before the time adverbial and the (poetic) object at the end.
segments.s439-g16: نه is the negative/coordinating 'nor/not'. شاهان شوند is a clause with شاهان 'kings' as subject and شوند (شوند) here expressing 'will become / will be made' (3rd pl. subj./future sense). از تو indicates origin/source 'from you'. زین پس is an idiom 'henceforth/after this' (زین = 'this', پس = 'after'). به بیم 'out of fear' gives the reason/manner. Together: 'Nor will kings be made from you henceforth, through fear.'
segments.s439-g65: برادر is the subject ('the brother'). ز + تیرش is a prepositional phrase meaning 'because of/from his arrow' with the enclitic -ش marking possession on تیر. بترسید is a simple past verb (classical form with b‑ prefix) meaning 'feared'. سخت is an adverb placed after the verb to intensify it ('greatly/strongly').
segments.s439-g66: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'he came'. سپر کرد is a verb + object construction 'made (a) shield' (کرد = 'made/did' with سپر as its complement). تن را places 'the body' as a definite object marked by را ('for the body / to protect the body'). درخت at the end names the material/thing used — 'the tree' (i.e. 'he made the tree a shield').
segments.s439-g67: درختی is an indefinite noun 'a tree' (the -ی giving an indefinite/qualifying sense). بدید is the past tense 'he saw.' از برابر is a prepositional phrase where از + برابر gives 'from/opposite' (i.e. 'opposite to'), and چنار names the plane tree opposite which the tree stood.
segments.s439-g68: بروبر is an adverbial/compound meaning 'face‑to‑face' or 'directly opposite.' گذشته is a past participle/adjectival use meaning 'having passed/been there' (here describing duration). بسی روزگار is a quantifying phrase 'many a time/long ages' — بسی intensifies روزگار ('many/ much time/ages'). Together they describe that the tree had stood there for many years.
segments.s439-g69: میانش = میان + enclitic -ش 'its middle/inside.' تهی qualifies it as 'empty' and بار ('load/fruit') specifies what it is empty of ('empty of fruit/load'). و links the next clause; برگش here is from برگشتن in an archaic/contracted form meaning 'it returned/was restored' (i.e. the leaves returned), and بجای means 'into place' or 'to their place.' The clause describes the tree's internal hollowness and the leaves being in place.
segments.s439-g70: نهان شد is a past passive/medio construction 'became hidden' / 'hid.' پسش is an adverbial phrase 'behind it' (پس + enclitic ـش = 'behind that'). مرد ناپاک رای is a noun phrase 'man of impure / foul counsel' — ناپاک modifies مرد and رای ('counsel/intent') specifies the kind of man (treacherous, ill‑intentioned).
segments.s439-g71: چو is a subordinating conjunction 'when.' رستم is the subject (proper name). چنان refers back to the situation just seen ('that/so'), and دید is past 'saw.' بفراخت is an older past form with the b‑ prefix meaning 'he thrust up/roused/brandished' — here 'he raised/brandished his hand.' دست is the direct object 'hand' placed after the verb.
segments.s439-g72: چنان خسته 'so / thus weary' is an adverb + adjective phrase modifying the following action. از تیر 'from the arrow' gives the cause of the weariness. بگشاد is a past/formal verb 'he opened' (classical b‑ prefix) and shَد/گشاد here governs شست (thumb) — 'he opened/unclenched his thumb,' i.e. uncurled his grip. This describes a physical loosening because of fatigue from the arrow.
segments.s439-g41: همه (all/everyone) is a universal quantifier placed before the noun; شهریاران (plural of شهریار, “rulers/kings”) is the head noun; ایران here specifies whose rulers (a genitival apposition) and follows the noun. بدند is an archaic past copular/auxiliary (3rd plural) meaning “they were” (classical Persian uses ـدَند/بدند as past). Together the clause literally reads “all the rulers of Iran were.”
segments.s439-g42: به + رزم marks direction/purpose “to (the) battle”; اندرون is a locative “inside/within” and here sets the locus (the household/inner ranks). نره شیران is a compound where نره is an adjective/epithet “manly/virile” modifying شیران (here used metaphorically for brave men); بدند again is the past 3rd plural copula/auxiliary (‘they were’/‘they went’ in context of being present in the action). Word order is flexible/poetic, but the core is “(those) manly lions within went to battle.”
segments.s439-g43: برفتند is the simple past 3rd-person plural of رفتن “to go/leave” (“they departed”). و is the coordinating conjunction “and”; ما is the 1st-person plural subject “we”; دیرتر is the comparative adverb “later”; ماندیم is the past 1st-person plural of ماندن “to remain/stay.” Word order is subject–adverb–verb in the second clause (ما دیرتر ماندیم = ‘we stayed later’).
segments.s439-g44: چو is a comparative/simile particle here meaning “like/as”; شیر is the simile’s noun “lion”; ژیان (an older form of زیان/زیستن-related root) is used as ‘life’ or ‘the course of life’; برگذر here is adjectival/verbal in sense “passed by/past” (literally ‘having passed’); ماندیم repeats the past 1st-plural “we remained.” The whole simile reads “we remained like a lion whose life had passed.”
segments.s439-g45: فرامرز is a proper name; پور here is the patronymic particle meaning “son (of)” and links the name to the following epithet; جهان‌بین is an epithet/compound meaning literally “world-seer” (a descriptive title); من at the end is a possessive reference “my” (classical Persian often places the possessor after the name or title for emphasis). The phrase is appositional: “Faramarz, son of Jahān-bīn, my (son/man).”
segments.s439-g46: بیاید is a 3rd-person jussive/subjunctive meaning “may (he) come / let (him) come”; بخواهد is another 3rd-person subjunctive meaning “may (he) desire / may (he) wish / may (he) seek.” The sequence بیاید بخواهد is a classical hortative construction meaning “let him come and seek.” ز introduces the object-source “from/against you”; تو is the 2nd-person pronoun; کین means “vengeance/hatred” and is the thing to be demanded; من is the possessor here (“my vengeance”). The whole clause: “May he come and demand from you my vengeance.”
segments.s439-g47: چنین گفت is the fixed phrase “thus he said / he spoke thus” (چنین = “thus/so”; گفت is the past 3rd-person singular of گفتن “to say” in classical narration). پس is an adverb “then/thereupon”; با before a personal name can mean “to/with” in the sense of addressing someone, so با شغاد پلید here means “to the vile Shaghād” (شغاد is a proper name in the Shahnameh; پلید is an epithet “vile/base”). The speaker is addressing Shaghād and what follows is reported speech.
segments.s439-g48: The clause begins with the complementizer/quotative که introducing the reported content; اکنون = “now”; the repeated که is emphatic/colloquial classical repetition (roughly “that now that…”); بر is the preposition “upon/on” marking the experiencer; من is the 1st-person object “me”; چنین = “thus/so”; بد = “bad/evil”; رسید is the past 3rd-person singular of رسیدن used idiomatically “befell/has reached (someone).” Together: “that now such ill has befallen me.”
segments.s439-g57: ندرد is a negative-form verb from دریدن used here in the sense 'will not be torn' (a stative/passive meaning). مگر introduces the exception 'except/unless'. ژنده شیری is a terse poetic phrase: شیری 'lion(-like)' qualifies ژنده (a damaged or ragged thing), together giving the image of a lion’s tearing; تنم 'my body' is the patient (object) attached as a suffixed pronoun.
segments.s439-g58: زمانی بود is a temporal clause 'there was a time' (زمانی = 'once/at one time', بود = past of 'to be'). تن به خاک افگنم is an old/poetic order meaning 'I would cast my body to the earth' (تن = 'body', به خاک = 'to the earth', افگنم is an archaic subjunctive/optative form of افکندن ‘to throw/lay down’). The clause expresses a past readiness or habit.
segments.s439-g59: The nominal subject شغاد (a dart/arrow name) is followed by آمد 'came' — simple narrative sequence. آن چرخ را is a definite/marked object ('that wheel'), with را marking the object; برکشید is a perfective verb 'drew up/raised' or, in a weapons context, 'drew' (i.e. pulled back). The order places agent-action after the initial noun phrase in typical epic narrative style.
segments.s439-g60: به زه کرد is a light-verb construction: به زه ('to the string') + کرد (did/made) = 'he set it to the bow-string'. و links clauses. یک بارش اندر کشید breaks down as یک بار 'one time/one shot' + ـش (possessive 'its' or object clitic) + اندر کشید 'drew in/away' — here 'کشید' means 'drew (the arrow)'. The sequence describes fitting the arrow and releasing/drawing once.
segments.s439-g61: بخندید is simple past 'he laughed'. و links the actions. پیش تهمتن نهاد is 'placed before Tahmētan' or 'put (it) in front of Tahmētan' — پیش = 'in the presence of/before', تهمتن is the hero's name, and نهاد is the past of نهادن 'to put/place'. The verb nهاد governs the object implied from the previous clauses.
segments.s439-g62: به مرگ برادر همی بود شاد is a predicative clause 'he was glad at the death of his brother'. به introduces the cause/target ('at/for the death of'), مرگ برادر = 'the brother's death', همی بود is the imperfective past auxiliary construction 'was (indeed/then)', and شاد = 'glad'. The order places the reason before the copula and predicate, typical of older Persian.
segments.s439-g63: تهمتن (the hero) is the subject. به سختی is an adverbial phrase 'with difficulty/with great effort' (به + سختی). کمان برگرفت is 'took up / raised the bow' — برگرفت is the perfective past of برگرفتن 'to take up'. The sequence describes the strenuous physical action.
segments.s439-g64: بدان خستگی is a causal/temporal phrase 'from/with that fatigue' (بدان = به + آن 'by/with that', خستگی = 'fatigue'). تیرش اندر گرفت means 'he seized/took his arrow' (تیرش = 'his arrow', اندر گرفت = 'took in/into [hand]' — گرفت here 'seized/took'). Together: because of that weariness he took hold of his arrow.
segments.s439-g73: This is a short nominal + verbal clause with the verb at the end (poetic word order). درخت (tree) and برادر (brother) are joined by the conjunction و; بهم is a reciprocal/adverbial meaning 'to one another' and بر (here) is a prepositional element forming the compound sense with the verb بدوخت (sewed). بدوخت is a past tense verb 'sewed', so the whole segment reads '[the] tree and [the] brother sewed to one another / were sewn together.'
segments.s439-g74: به هنگام + verbal noun رفتن forms a temporal phrase 'at the time of going' (here رفتن functions as 'dying/departing'). دلش is the noun دل 'heart' with the possessive clitic ش 'his'. برفروخت is a past tense poetic verb (برفروختن) used figuratively 'was kindled/was set aflame' or 'was inflamed' describing his emotional state at that time.
segments.s439-g75: Subject شغاد appears first (here a proper name in the narrative), followed by the temporal/prepositional phrase از پس زخم او 'after/from the wound of him' (i.e. after his wound), and the light‑noun + verb آه کرد 'gave a sigh / sighed' where کرد is the light verb. The overall syntax is Subject + adverbial phrase + predicate 'sighed'.
segments.s439-g76: تهمتن is the epithet/name (Tahmatan) as subject; کوتاه کرد is a compound verb 'to make short / to cut short' where کرد is the past light verb and کوتاه is the adjective 'short/brief' used as part of the predicate 'shortened/ended the pain'. درد is the direct object 'the pain'. The particle/word برو here is a poetic/elliptical connective meaning roughly 'thereby/thereupon' (not the ordinary imperative 'go'), so the clause reads 'Tahmatan thereby shortened/ended the pain.'
segments.s439-g77: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him/unto him' (indirect object). گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (here used with subject following it — inverted order), and رستم is the subject. ز یزدان سپاس is a short prepositional phrase 'thanks from God' idiomatically 'thanks be to God.' Note: word order is verb + subject + complement for poetic emphasis.
segments.s439-g78: که introduces a content clause (complement) explaining what was said. بودم is first‑person past 'I was / I have been'. همه‌ساله in this context functions idiomatically as 'through all the years / always,' and یزدان‑شناس is a predicate noun 'God‑knowing / pious person.' The clause therefore means 'that I have always been God‑knowing.'
segments.s439-g79: ازان پس که is a temporal sequence 'from that time when / since that after'. جانم is 'my life' (جان + the possessive clitic -م 'my'), and رسیده به لب is a verbal phrase 'has reached the lip/brink' — an idiom meaning 'has come to the very brink' (i.e. near death). Grammatically this is a subordinate temporal clause 'since that time when my life had reached the brink.'
segments.s439-g80: برین here is the fused phrase بَر این 'on/for this' (the preposition بر + demonstrative این), introducing the reason 'because of this'; کین من is 'my vengeance/grudge' with من marking possession. روز نامد به شب is a compact poetic clause meaning 'day did not come/turn into night' — i.e. time did not pass as usual. Altogether: 'because of this vengeance of mine, day did not pass into night.'

sentences.s439-s9: If the time should come upon you, you will be slain in the trap of the Ahrimans.
sentences.s439-s10: At that moment the commander of Kabul came from the road across the plain into the hunting-ground.
sentences.s439-s11: He saw Piltan so exhausted; he saw that very weariness unbound.
sentences.s439-s12: He said to him, 'O renowned commander of the army, what were you doing on this plain, in the hunting-ground?'
sentences.s439-s13: I will soon bring a few physicians; because of your pain I will shed blood‑stained tears.
sentences.s439-s14: Unless your weariness is made right, you must not wash my face with blood‑water.
sentences.s439-s15: Tahmatan thus gave an answer to him, saying: 'O man of bad character, seeker of a cure.'
sentences.s439-s16: My time has come, O physician; do not smear me with bloody tears.
sentences.s439-s41: You forced me, and before death I sought vengeance on this faithless one.
sentences.s439-s42: He said this, and his soul left his body; the assembly became stricken and wept.
sentences.s439-s43: Zowāreh died in another well; no horseman remained among the great and the wise.
sentences.s439-s1: When, with weariness, his eyes opened, he saw the face of that ill‑minded one, Shaghad.
sentences.s439-s2: He realized that this was his remedy and way; Shaghad is deceptive and malevolent toward him.
sentences.s439-s3: He said to him, 'O unfortunate and accursed man! Because of your deeds the once‑prosperous land has been ruined.'
sentences.s439-s4: Regret will come to you from these words; turn away from this evil, that you do not grow old.
sentences.s439-s25: Take my bow from his quiver; put that interpreter to use for me.
sentences.s439-s26: String it and set it before me with two arrows; it should not be that that lion, the hunter...
sentences.s439-s27: From the plain he comes in for the hunt; I have fallen here — thus (I am) a body‑piercer (tormentor).
sentences.s439-s28: If he sees me, harm will come from him to me; a bow would be useful to me.
sentences.s439-s17: You will not remain long; when the time comes, no one will pass away alive from Basman.
sentences.s439-s18: Nor do I possess any greater farr than Jamshid's — so that Biwar might seize it from among them, if ...
sentences.s439-s19: Not from Afridun, nor from Kay Qobad — the great ones and the kings of fortunate birth.
sentences.s439-s20: Siavash's throat was cut with a dagger; when his time came, his cuirass/armor yielded.
sentences.s439-s5: Go with Faramarz and be single-minded; with your life and heart be his well-wisher.
sentences.s439-s6: Thus the wretch Shaghad answered that the revolving heavens had given you up.
sentences.s439-s7: How many times have you burst forth to shed blood — pillaging and hanging everywhere?
sentences.s439-s8: From Kabul you will no longer seek silver; nor will kings be made from you henceforth, out of fear.
sentences.s439-s33: The brother was greatly afraid of his arrow; he came and used the tree as a shield for his body.
sentences.s439-s34: He saw a tree opposite the plane tree that had stood there through many ages.
sentences.s439-s35: Its interior was empty of fruit and its leaves had returned to their place; then the foul‑minded man hid behind it.
sentences.s439-s36: When Rostam saw that, he raised his hand; so exhausted from the arrow, he opened his thumb (uncurled his hand).
sentences.s439-s21: All the rulers of Iran were present; the manly lions within went to battle.
sentences.s439-s22: They departed, and we stayed behind later; like a lion whose life had passed, we remained.
sentences.s439-s23: My Faramarz, son of Jahān-bīn, may come and demand from you my vengeance.
sentences.s439-s24: Then he said thus to the vile Shaghād: “Now that such ill has befallen me...”
sentences.s439-s29: My body will not be torn except by a lion's tearing; there was a time I would cast my body to the earth.
sentences.s439-s30: A Shaghad (dart) came; he drew up that wheel, set it to the string and pulled one shot.
sentences.s439-s31: He laughed and placed (it) before Tahmētan; he was glad at his brother’s death.
sentences.s439-s32: Tahmētan, with great effort, took up the bow; from that weariness he seized his arrow.
sentences.s439-s37: He sewed the tree and his brother together; at the moment of departing his heart was inflamed.
sentences.s439-s38: Shaghād sighed after his wound; Tahmatan thereby put an end to the pain.
sentences.s439-s39: Rostam said to him, 'Thanks to God,' (adding) 'that I have, through all my years, been God‑knowing.'
sentences.s439-s40: From that time, when my life had come to my lips, because of this vengeance of mine the day did not pass into night.
